name: Al Jarreau
abstract: Alwin Lopez Jarreau (March 12, 1940 – February 12, 2017) was an American singer. His 1981 album Breakin' Away spent two years on the Billboard 200 and is considered one of the finest examples of the Los Angeles pop and R&B sound.

about_quotes.speaker: Jerry Hey
about_quotes.evidence: All the Jarreau horn parts were — I don’t think we played a note before eleven maybe... And on the Jarreau record we had already been booked during the day.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jerry Hey interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, November 2, 2023

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Golson
about_quotes.evidence: Al Jarreau’s going to sing Whisper Not, which he recorded with us on the new CD.
about_quotes.doc_title: Benny Golson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Luis Conte
about_quotes.evidence: The reason why they call them is because it’s a section. And they breathe a certain way and they play together and it’s just the beauty of the thing. “You guys are lining this shit up? What?” ... The reason why they call those four guys to do all these record dates that are so funky on Al Jarreau or
about_quotes.doc_title: Luis Conte interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, November 24, 2025

about_quotes.speaker: George Benson
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee states 'We’re very, very good friends' referring to Al Schmitt, not Al Jarreau. Al Jarreau is only mentioned by the interviewer as the person through whom the interviewer knows Al Schmitt.
about_quotes.doc_title: George Benson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Rick Montalbano
about_quotes.evidence: INTERVIEWER: I remember you making a comment once about, I think it was Al Jarreau... INTERVIEWEE: Yeah. I guess. I don't remember specifically saying it. I probably did.
about_quotes.doc_title: Rick Montalbano interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, August 12, 2011

about_quotes.speaker: Hubert Laws
about_quotes.evidence: Al Jarreau is mentioned by the Interviewer in a list of artists/albums, but the Interviewee does not respond to or acknowledge any personal connection with Al Jarreau.
about_quotes.doc_title: Hubert Laws — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
